source_documents: https://www.rbi.org.in (Master Directions on Frauds - Classification & Reporting, incl. Early Warning Signals)
category: Credit fraud - falsified income/documents to obtain a loan
risk_signals: Income proofs that do not reconcile with bank-statement cash flows or tax records
Inconsistent or templated KYC documents, often routed through a third-party facilitator/agent
Default on the very first instalments, indicating no genuine repayment intent
analyst_reasoning: Application fraud inflates or fabricates the borrower's capacity to repay so a loan is sanctioned that never should have been. The documents are internally inconsistent because they are manufactured, and first-payment default reveals the intent - which is exactly what early-warning-signal frameworks are designed to catch.
investigation_steps: Reconcile declared income against bank statements, tax filings, and employer verification
Check documents for templating, reuse, or a common facilitator across applications
Flag first-payment or very-early defaults for fraud review, not just collections
decision: Reject or recall the facility, file a fraud report under the frauds-classification framework, and review the originating agent's other files.
outcome: Fraudulent loans crystallise as early NPAs; document reconciliation at underwriting is the cheapest point to stop them.
lessons_learned: Internal inconsistency between income proofs and cash flows is the core application-fraud signal
First-payment default should trigger a fraud review, not just collections
A shared facilitator across many suspect files points to an organised ring
